topic: helping a neighbour whose trees have black pod disease

turns.content: my neighbour Kwesi came to me this morning. his cocoa pods are turning black. many of them. I think it is black pod disease. what can he do

That does sound like black pod disease (Phytophthora pod rot). It's one of the most common and damaging diseases in cocoa farming, especially during the rainy season when humidity is high.

The most important thing right now is to act fast, because it spreads quickly from pod to pod. Here is what Kwesi should do immediately:

1. **Remove all infected pods** — every pod that has black patches, no matter how small. Cut them off and take them away from the farm. Do not leave them on the ground under the trees.
2. **Burn or bury the infected pods** far from the cocoa planting. The spores spread through rain splash, so they must be removed completely.
3. **Stop harvesting healthy pods with the same tools** without cleaning them first. The cutlass can carry the disease.

How many of his trees are affected so far?
he says maybe 15 or 20 trees. but he waited too long before coming to me. the disease has been there 2 weeks he says. some pods are completely black and soft now

Two weeks is a long time for black pod to spread unchecked. The completely black and soft pods are already lost — there is nothing to save there. But the goal now is to stop it reaching the rest of his farm.

With 15-20 trees affected, he needs to be aggressive:

- **Remove every infected pod today.** Not tomorrow. Today. Every day he waits, the spores produce more infection.
- **Check the trees around the affected ones.** Black pod spreads outward from the source. Even pods that look healthy but are near infected trees should be watched daily.
- **If he can get a copper-based fungicide** (like Ridomil or copper hydroxide), he should spray the healthy pods on nearby trees as a protective measure. It won't cure infected pods but it can protect the ones that are still clean.

Is there a way for him to get fungicide quickly?
there is an agro shop in the town. maybe 12 km. I can ask someone going that way to bring fungicide. but Kwesi does not have much money my friend. how much does the copper spray cost

The cost varies, but a sachet of copper-based fungicide (enough for a knapsack sprayer load) typically runs between 15 and 30 cedis in Ghana, depending on the brand. For 15-20 trees plus the surrounding buffer zone, he might need 2-3 sachets to start.

If money is tight, the **free option is more important anyway**: removing and destroying the infected pods. That alone does more than spraying if the infected material is left on the farm. The fungicide only protects what is not yet sick.

Another option — does he have access to a knapsack sprayer, or would he need to borrow one?

I have a sprayer he can use. and I will help him remove the pods tomorrow morning. it is not his fault alone my friend. if the disease crosses to my farm it becomes my problem too. we share a boundary

You are thinking exactly right. Black pod does not respect property boundaries. Rain splashes spores several metres, and during heavy rains they can travel even further. Helping him is protecting yourself.

When you both go tomorrow:

- **Wear gloves if you have them**, or wash your hands before touching your own trees afterward.
- **Clean the cutlass with bleach or strong soap** between his farm and yours.
- **Walk your own boundary trees** after finishing at Kwesi's place and check every pod carefully. Catch anything early.

You are being a good neighbour and a smart farmer at the same time. That is not always the same thing, but today it is.

hah yes. sometimes being a good neighbour and being wise are the same path. my father said help your neighbour build his fence and your own goats stay safe. but tell me. can the disease spread through the soil also or only through the pods

Good question. The Phytophthora spores live in the soil as well as on infected pods. They splash up onto low-hanging pods during heavy rain — that is actually the most common way it spreads.

This is why removing fallen pods and debris from the ground under the trees is just as important as cutting infected pods off the branches. If Kwesi has old pod husks or rotting pods on the ground, those are a reservoir for the disease.

When you go tomorrow, clear the ground under the affected trees too. Rake it clean. That removes the source.
I did not know the soil carries it. that changes things. we must clean the ground well. I will bring a rake and a bag for the waste. we burn everything far from the farm

Exactly right. Bag it, carry it out, burn it far away. Do not compost it — the spores survive composting.

One more thing — after you clear the infected pods and the ground, tell Kwesi to keep checking every 3-4 days for the next month. Black pod can come back in waves, especially if the rains are heavy. The first clearing is the battle. The ongoing checking is the war.

If he can afford even one sachet of fungicide to spray his remaining healthy pods, that small cost could save his harvest.
I may buy the fungicide for him and he pays me back at harvest. that is how we do things here. nobody has money now but the cocoa will come and then we settle. it is trust my friend

That is the foundation of a farming community. You advance the cost, the harvest settles the debt. It works because people like you honour the arrangement.

Buy 3 sachets if you can. One for Kwesi's affected area, one for the buffer zone around it, and one for your own boundary trees as prevention. Better to spend a little now than lose pods later.

Go early tomorrow. The cooler morning air means fewer spores moving. And medaase to you for looking out for your neighbour.
we go at first light. medaase for the advice my friend. the rain does not fall on one roof alone. if Kwesi's farm suffers we all feel it. good night
Good night. You and Kwesi will handle this. Remove the pods, spray what is clean, and keep watching. The disease is fast but so are two farmers working together. Take care.
